insights: All five agents have exactly 100 assignments over the 13-month period, indicating a perfectly balanced total workload distribution with no agent bearing disproportionate cumulative responsibility.

Charlie Whitherspoon shows the only statistically detectable upward trend in monthly assignments (+2.0 per month on average), increasing from 6.7 to 8.7 assignments/month across the first and last three months — yet this remains within normal operational volatility (5–14 assignments/month) and does not reflect overload.

Howard Johnson exhibits a weaker upward trend (slope=0.14, correlation=0.18), but his monthly assignment volume shows no meaningful deviation from baseline and remains fully consistent with system-wide stability.
No agent exceeds 14 assignments in any single month, and all monthly ranges fall within expected operational variation — confirming absence of sustained or acute workload pressure.

The uniformity of total assignments (100 each) combined with low inter-agent variance in monthly volume indicates deliberate and effective assignment governance, with no evidence of systemic drift toward imbalance.
summary: The analysis confirms no overload situations exist among the five agents: each has exactly 100 assignments over 13 months, and monthly volumes remain tightly bounded (5–14 per agent), reflecting stable, equitable workload distribution. While Charlie Whitherspoon shows the strongest upward trend (+2.0 assignments/month), this change is modest, statistically weak (r=0.40), and fully contained within normal operational fluctuation — not indicative of emerging risk. The system demonstrates robust assignment control with no evidence of concentration, drift, or bottleneck. Next focus should be validating whether the observed minor trend for Charlie aligns with intentional role expansion or temporary coverage needs, rather than operational concern.
